one_line_takeaway: By sanitizing private root values once and deriving every later response as pure post-processing, RootGuard makes an LLM agent's differential-privacy guarantee immune to multi-turn query accumulation, cutting target error 2.6-3.8x and blocking MAP reconstruction attacks that degrade independent per-release noising by over 3x as adversarial query count grows.
key_finding: RootGuard (M-Opt) achieves 2.6x lower aggregate target-error (wMAPE) than independent noising (M-All) at epsilon=0.1, B=(2k+1)epsilon (7.8% vs 20.3%, Exponential mechanism), widening to 3.8x at B=(3k+1)epsilon (5.6% vs 21.5%); this widening 'double asymmetry' occurs because M-All's utility depends only on epsilon (not the larger multi-turn budget B) while RootGuard's utility improves with every additional turn since the whole budget concentrates on the k roots. These numeric gains translate to clinical Risk Class Error reductions of 2.2x at (2k+1)epsilon (7.0% vs 15.7%) widening to 3.3x at (3k+1)epsilon (5.3% vs 17.5%). Under the MAP reconstruction attack (RQ2, matched per-root noise epsilon_r=0.1, informed prior, Strategy B all-roots), M-All's reconstruction wMAPE drops (i.e. the adversary reconstructs the true root values more accurately) from 10.2% to 3.2% as adversarial query count q grows from 1 to 16, a 3.2x tightening of the adversary's estimate, while RootGuard (M-Roots/M-Opt) remains flat/invariant at 13.2%/11.9% across all q -- because RootGuard's cached responses give the adversary no new observations after the first release. The per-template structural analysis (RQ3) shows RootGuard wins on every (template, mechanism, epsilon) cell tested, with the largest multiplicative/absolute gains on templates whose target formula compresses root noise (AIP, HOMA, FIB4, NLR: 2.1-3.3x at focal epsilon=0.1, B=(2k+1)epsilon) versus smaller absolute (but still 2.3-3.3x multiplicative) gains on noise-amplifying formulas (ANEMIA, CONICITY, TYG, VASCULAR). The sensitivity-weighted allocation empirically follows the predicted power law epsilon_i proportional to (|h_i|*D_i)^0.5 with fitted slope 0.50, mechanism-independent across all three DP mechanisms tested. The end-to-end LLM deployment (RQ4, GPT-5.4 nano, 21,600 sessions) reproduces the mechanism-level ordering M-All > M-Roots > M-Opt at every cell, with M-Opt achieving 7.6% vs M-All's 22.2% wMAPE at the focal cell (2.9x, agreeing with the mechanism-level 2.6x within 1.9pp across all cells), zero session-level parsing/compliance failures, confirming the theoretical/mechanism-level results transfer to a realistic agentic tool-call deployment. The CAPE-F text-DP baseline achieves only 263-403% wMAPE (65-125x worse than the paper's Bounded-Laplace-Opt configuration) and stays flat across privacy levels, which the authors use to argue that text-based DP mechanisms are fundamentally unsuited to structured numeric medical data.
core_contribution: RootGuard, a dependency-aware sanitization mechanism for LLM agents that release private user data (via metric differential privacy, mDP) to untrusted services across multi-turn interactions. Instead of applying independent mDP noise to every value a service requests (the prior-art approach, e.g. Preempt and CAPE), RootGuard noises each declared private 'root' value exactly once at initialization, caches the noised value, and answers all subsequent requests (repeated queries, derived-function queries, and the final task query) as deterministic post-processing of the cached noised roots. By the post-processing theorem of DP, this means the total privacy guarantee never degrades with additional turns, adversary-chosen functions, or repeated queries -- unlike independent per-release noising. The paper also formalizes and proves (Theorem 2) that independently noising a root and a nonlinear function of that root amplifies the root's distinguishability by up to the function's Lipschitz constant, and shows (Theorem 3) that derived values inherit privacy for free ('Implied Privacy'). Beyond the core mechanism, RootGuard exploits two levels of public structural knowledge when available: (1) inter-root dependencies (e.g. BMI = f(height, weight)) to avoid noising redundant roots, and (2) knowledge of the target computation's form to allocate the DP budget across roots via a closed-form sensitivity-weighted rule (epsilon_i proportional to sqrt(|h_i| * D_i), where h_i is the partial derivative of the known intermediate function g at the population mean and D_i is the root's domain width).
threat_model: A user agent U holds private root values X=(x1,...,xk) and must interact with an untrusted/adversarial service A (an LLM tool-call recipient, e.g. a diagnostic service in an MCP-style agent pipeline) that computes a task function T(X) on sanitized inputs. A may be malicious by design, compromised via prompt injection (citing Greshake et al., InjecAgent, AgentDojo), or coerced/breached, and is modeled as capable of, over t interaction turns: requesting undisclosed root values, forcing computation of arbitrary attacker-chosen functions f(z1,...,zd) on already-released values, and re-requesting already-disclosed values. The adversary has full knowledge of the sanitization scheme's parameters and may hold population-level priors, and in the final turn computes the target function T using values it has extracted. The adversary's concrete attack strategy evaluated is Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) reconstruction of the true root values from one or more independently noised observations. This is a genuine security/privacy paper: it defines a formal privacy game (G_DAG), states and proves cryptographic-style advantage bounds (Theorem 1: adversary advantage bounded by root-only sanitization parameters, independent of adversary's function choices), and evaluates against an explicit adversarial reconstruction attack -- not merely a robustness or accuracy benchmark.
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) they assume perfect Named Entity Recognition for identifying which prompt tokens are sensitive roots, following prior sanitizers (Preempt, CAPE) -- integrating noisy/imperfect NER into the formal privacy analysis is left open; (2) evaluation covers only structured numeric values in single-user sessions -- free-form text, multi-user composition/collusion, additional LLM backbones, and nonlinear derived-function reconstruction attacks (which the authors' own Theorem 2 predicts would further amplify M-All's vulnerability) are left to future work, meaning the reported reconstruction numbers are explicitly framed as a conservative lower bound on independent-noising's vulnerability; (3) the RQ2 MAP attack setup uses direct root queries only, not the (theoretically worse) derived-function queries; (4) the RQ4 deployment harness covers only the Exponential mechanism, three epsilon levels, and a single LLM backbone (GPT-5.4 nano); (5) the sensitivity-weighted allocation (M-Opt) trades off average per-root reconstruction robustness for target-root accuracy -- on some templates (e.g. ANEMIA) M-Opt actually has higher average reconstruction wMAPE than uniform M-Roots because M-Opt starves low-sensitivity roots of budget, making them individually easier to reconstruct even though the aggregate task-relevant error is lower; (6) RootGuard requires the user agent to already know/declare which values are roots and, for its stronger 'Level 2' guarantee, requires public knowledge of the target intermediate function's form (e.g. a published clinical formula or MCP tool schema) -- this is a nontrivial deployment precondition rather than a fully automatic defense.

datasets_used: CDC NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey) 2017-2018, examination data: body measures, blood pressure, hematology, cholesterol, triglycerides, glucose, insulin; 200 adult male patients drawn per template (per-template subpopulation n=1,195 to 2,840) for RQ1-3, first 100 of these used for the RQ4 LLM deployment evaluation

8 constructed clinical diagnostic templates built from NHANES fields: ANEMIA (MCHC), AIP (log10(TG/HDL)), HOMA-IR (Glu*Ins/405), FIB4 (age*AST/(PLT*sqrt(ALT))), NLR (NEU/LYM), TYG (ln(TG*Glu/2)), CONICITY (waist/(0.109*sqrt(wt/ht))), VASCULAR (100*(SBP-DBP)/SBP)
quantitative_results: wMAPE at eps=0.1, B=(2k+1)eps, Exponential mechanism: M-Opt 7.8% vs M-All 20.3% (2.6x improvement)
wMAPE at eps=0.1, B=(3k+1)eps: M-Opt 5.6% vs M-All 21.5% (3.8x improvement)
Risk Class Error at eps=0.1, B=(2k+1)eps: M-Opt 7.0%+/-0.6% vs M-All 15.7%+/-0.8% (2.2x); widens to 3.3x at (3k+1)eps (5.3% vs 17.5%)
MAP reconstruction wMAPE (RQ2, eps_r=0.1, informed prior, Strategy B): M-All drops from 10.2% (q=1) to 3.2% (q=16); M-Roots/M-Opt flat at 13.2%/11.9%

MAP reconstruction Strategy A (single-root worst case, uniform prior): M-All drops from 16.5% (q=1) to 5.9% (q=16), a 2.8x reduction attributable to independent noise averaging; M-Roots flat at 22.4%, M-Opt flat at 15.1%

Per-template wMAPE ratios at eps=0.1, B=(2k+1)eps (Exponential): AIP 41.9%->12.7% (3.3x), HOMA 31.1%->9.9% (3.1x), NLR 49.2%->23.1% (2.1x), FIB4 23.9%->10.0% (2.4x), VASCULAR 6.3%->2.6% (2.4x), TYG 4.6%->2.0% (2.3x), CONICITY 2.8%->1.0% (2.8x), ANEMIA 2.4%->0.7% (3.3x); no template below 2.1x, growing to 3.0-5.4x at (3k+1)eps
Sensitivity-weighted allocation power law: fitted slope 0.50 (matching theoretical eps_i ~ (|h_i|*D_i)^0.5), mechanism-independent (Exp/BLap/Stair all ~0.49-0.52 at eps=0.5)

RQ4 LLM deployment (GPT-5.4 nano, 21,600 sessions, 162,000 observations): wMAPE at eps=0.1, B=(2k+1)eps: M-Opt 7.6%+/-0.5% vs M-All 22.2%+/-1.2% (2.9x), agreeing with RQ1's 2.6x within 1.9pp across all nine (eps,B) cells
RQ4 Risk Class Error at eps=0.1: M-Opt 7.3% vs M-All 19.2% at B=(2k+1)eps (2.6x reduction); widens to 3.6x at (3k+1)eps (5.4% vs 19.7%)
Zero session-level parse/compliance failures across 21,600 LLM deployment sessions
CAPE-F text-DP baseline: 263-403% wMAPE across all epsilon (65-125x worse than Bounded-Laplace-Opt), essentially flat across privacy levels
baselines_compared: M-All: independent per-release metric-DP noising (representative of prior stateless sanitizers such as Preempt and CAPE) -- this is the paper's primary comparison baseline
M-Roots: RootGuard variant with uniform (non-sensitivity-weighted) budget allocation across roots -- an ablation isolating the gain from eliminating redundant noising
M-Opt: full RootGuard with sensitivity-weighted budget allocation -- the paper's proposed complete method

CAPE-F: CAPE (Wu et al. 2025) text-DP mechanism adapted to numeric values by constraining decoding to numeric characters, included as a reference to show text-based DP is unsuited to structured numeric medical data
Three underlying DP noise mechanisms tested for all configurations: Discrete Exponential, Bounded Laplace, Staircase
